sections.heading: First Sector
sections.body:
On the run down to the first corner, La Source, ensure you are all the way over to the left side of the track and spot your braking point at the 100-meter board. If you remember the chaos of the 2012 Belgian Grand Prix, this corner can be quite the first-lap incident hotspot, so make sure you are very aware of where your braking point is if you have other cars around you. Turn in early and try to keep everything in one smooth motion to give you the best rundown towards Eau Rouge.

Once La Source is ticked off, you will be hurtling down towards what is arguably one of the most famous corner sequences in international motorsport, Eau Rouge and Raidillion. Positioned as close to the wall as you dare on the straight as you can, hit the first apex on the small kerb on the left before swinging the car back over to the right, flat out, over the crest. Once you have made it over the top, straighten out your car as much as possible to get the best run through Kemmel corner and onto the Kemmel Straight, thus closing out sector 1.

sections.heading: Sector 2
sections.body:
On the approach to Les Coombes, the right-left chicane at the end of the Kemmel Straight, position your car on the left and brake between the one-hundred-meter and fifty-meter boards, making sure to trail brake to carry as much speed as possible over the top of the kerb on the first apex. This kerb is harmless and will benefit you massively if you can attack it.

On the left-hand section of the chicane, be careful not to take too much kerb, as the white line dictates the track limit. If abused, it will result in your lap time being invalidated or, potentially worse, if it is not your first warning. Ensure the car is positioned to the left when setting up for the ninety-degree right turn, known as Malmedy. This will lead you onto a short straight before diving into the second hairpin on the track.

A prime low-speed overtaking spot, be cautious of other cars when navigating this corner. It can also be easy to get on the throttle too early and spin coming out of the exit kerb. Keep your car pinned to the inside kerb, and as soon as that kerb starts falling away, start accelerating and look to move your car over to the right for the braking zone of Speakers Corner.

Cut in late and run the car out wide as you make your way through Speakers Corner, keep the car on the right and lift ever so slightly to settle the car as you fire through turns ten and eleven at what will feel like Mach 10 speeds!

Turns twelve and thirteen are another combination of a right-left chicane. Brake at the end of the red kerb for twelve and ensure you do not mount the kerb on your left side too much as you swing the car around to aim down the short straight towards the final corner of Sector 2.

Turn fourteen in fifteen is vital for keeping momentum. The long run to the final few corners could be ruined if you drop too much speed, so make sure you are consistent through here at all costs. Once you get through fourteen, Sector 2 is over, and fifteen is flat out onto the back section of Sector 3.

sections.heading: Sector 3
sections.body:
The big test of sector three is the final tight and slow hairpin before the pit straight greets you to start another lap, but before that, the flat-out turns fifteen and sixteen are, again, essential to keep your momentum up. In GT cars or touring cars, these corners can be problematic without the necessary aero, but Formula One cars can handle these flat out with no problem at all.

Brake on the left side of the track at the one-hundred-meter board, making sure you are braking in a straight line, as you will be coming down from eighth gear right down to second. Hit the first apex as late as you can so you can straighten up the exit onto the pit straight to the best of your ability.
